title: Architecture Decisions Behind Building a Simple Personal Software Tool
summary:
How I moved from a traditional web application mindset to exploring local-first architecture

I wanted to build a simple software tool for my personal use.

Nothing complicated.

Something in the category of tools people build for themselves:

  • A personal expense tracker
  • A budgeting application
  • A private knowledge management tool
  • A personal organization system

The important characteristic was this:

The data belonged to one person.

It was not a social application.

It was not a collaboration platform.

It did not need users interacting with each other.

There was no requirement for:

  • Public profiles
  • Sharing updates
  • Real-time collaboration
  • Social features

It was simply a tool that helped one person manage their own information.

When I started thinking about building it, my first instinct was the most natural one for me.

I am a web application developer.

My comfort zone is building web applications.

So my first thought was:

"Why not build a Ruby on Rails application?"

Something like:

              User

               |

        Web Application

               |

        Ruby on Rails API

               |

        PostgreSQL Database

This is an architecture I have worked with many times.

The workflow is familiar:

  • Create models
  • Build controllers
  • Add authentication
  • Store data in a database
  • Deploy the application
  • Access it from anywhere

This is a proven architecture.

For many products, this is exactly the right approach.

But while thinking about this project, I asked myself a different question:

Am I choosing this architecture because the problem requires it, or because it is the architecture I already know?

That question changed the direction completely.

Understanding The Actual Problem

Before choosing technology, I wanted to understand the nature of the problem.

What kind of application was I actually building?

There is a big difference between building:

  • A social network
  • A marketplace
  • A collaboration platform
  • A communication application

versus building:

  • A personal tool
  • A private utility
  • A single-user productivity application

In the first category, the server is the center of the system.

Users need to communicate through it.

For example:

          User A

             |

             |

      Central Platform

             |

             |

          User B

The platform exists because users need each other.

The server is the meeting point.

But my problem was different.

The user did not need to interact with other users.

The user needed a tool.

That made me question something:

Does this application actually need to live on the internet?

The Default Web Application Architecture

As web developers, we naturally think in terms of:

              Client

                |

                |

            API Server

                |

                |

             Database

The database lives somewhere in the cloud.

The application talks to the backend.

The backend becomes the source of truth.

This model provides many advantages.

Access Anywhere

The user can access their information from different devices because the data exists remotely.

Central Updates

The developer can deploy a new version once, and everyone receives the update.

Multiple Users

The same system can serve thousands or millions of users.

But those benefits come with additional responsibilities.

The Hidden Complexity Behind "Just Add Login"

When building a SaaS application, one of the first things developers add is authentication.

It sounds simple:

"Just add login."

But authentication is only the beginning.

Authentication answers:

"Who are you?"

A simple flow:

User

 |

Login

 |

Identity Verification

 |

Access Granted

The system now knows who the user is.

But the next question is:

What is this user allowed to access?

Authorization

Authorization answers:

"What can this user do?"

Imagine a database containing information from multiple users:

              Database


        User A Data


        User B Data


        User C Data

The application must guarantee:

  • User A cannot read User B's data
  • User B cannot modify User C's data
  • Every request is properly checked

Now we need:

  • Permissions
  • Access rules
  • Security checks
  • Data isolation

Multi-tenancy

Once multiple customers use the same application, we enter the world of multi-tenancy.

A SaaS application is usually one application serving many customers.

A simple analogy is an apartment building.

The building is shared.

But every apartment is private.

             Application

                  |

        ---------------------

        Customer A Space

        Customer B Space

        Customer C Space

The application needs to ensure:

  • One customer's data never leaks to another
  • Customers are isolated
  • Database queries are safe
  • Updates work for everyone

Now the system has become a platform.

Again, this is not a bad thing.

This is exactly what SaaS companies need.

But I had to ask:

Does a personal software tool need all of this?

Infrastructure Responsibilities

A hosted application also introduces operational responsibilities:

  • Hosting
  • Database management
  • Backups
  • Monitoring
  • Deployments
  • Security updates
  • Scaling

These are important engineering problems.

But they are problems created by choosing a hosted multi-user architecture.

So I asked myself:

"Am I solving the user's problem, or am I solving problems created by my architecture choice?"

A Different Direction: What If The Application Lives On The Device?

This led me to consider another possibility.

What if the application was not primarily a web application?

What if it was a desktop application?

The architecture would become:

              User

               |

        Desktop Application

               |

        Local Database

Now many things become simpler.

The Device Becomes The Home Of The Data

Instead of:

"The application asks a server for my information"

the model becomes:

"The application already has my information."

The user's device becomes the primary place where data lives.

No Backend

There is no:

  • API server
  • Cloud database
  • Server infrastructure

The application communicates directly with local storage.

No Authentication

This was an interesting realization.

Why do applications need login systems?

Because they need to identify users accessing remote data.

A server needs to know:

"Who is requesting this information?"

But if the information is already on the user's own device, the operating system already knows the user.

There is no need to create:

  • Signup flows
  • Password management
  • Session handling
  • Account recovery

Better Privacy

The user's data stays on their device.

The application does not need to constantly send personal information to a remote server.

The user owns:

  • The application
  • The storage
  • The data

Less Operational Complexity

Removing the backend removes many responsibilities:

No:

  • Server maintenance
  • API versioning
  • Authentication systems
  • User management
  • Database scaling

The system becomes smaller.

And smaller systems are often easier to understand and maintain.

But Then Came The Real-World Problem

At this point, the desktop architecture looked attractive.

But then I asked myself:

"Would I personally use this only on my laptop?"

The answer was no.

Today, people use multiple devices.

A person might have:

              Laptop

                 |

                 |

              Phone

                 |

                 |

              Tablet

A desktop-only application creates a new problem.

The user now has multiple copies of their data.

The question becomes:

How do these devices stay in sync?

And this is where the architecture discussion became interesting.

Part 2: Designing synchronization without accidentally building another SaaS backend

Enjoyed this deep dive? Consider buying me a coffee ☕ on Superprofile to support more independent technical writing.

multimedia.caption: Imagen de portada del artículo

fullArticle:
Every software project starts with an idea.

Mine started with a surprisingly ordinary one.

I wanted to build a simple software tool for my own use.

Not a platform trying to connect millions of people.

Just a small application that quietly solved one problem in my daily life.

If you think about it, there are an entire category of applications that fall into this space.

Applications whose value comes from helping one person organise their own information.

That simple observation turned out to influence every architectural decision that followed.

I've spent most of my career building backend systems.

Naturally, when I think about building software, my brain reaches for the tools I know best.

If you've spent years building web applications, this thought process feels almost automatic...
